title: Building a safe, effective sandbox to enable Codex on Windows
content: Learn how OpenAI built a secure sandbox for Codex on Windows, enabling safe, efficient coding agents with controlled file access and network restrictions.
headline_ja: Windows版Codexを安全に動かすサンドボックス設計
overview: OpenAI News が、Windows 上で Codex を安全かつ効率的に動かすためのサンドボックス設計を紹介しています。対象は Codex のコーディングエージェント実行環境で、ファイルアクセスやネットワーク通信を制御しながら、実務利用に耐える安全性を確保する取り組みです。
whats_new: 今回の記事では、Windows 環境で Codex を実行する際に、安全なサンドボックスをどう構築したかを解説しています。具体的には、Codex に許可するファイルアクセス範囲を制限し、ネットワーク接続も厳格に抑制することで、コード実行時のリスクを低減しています。OpenAI はこの仕組みにより、エージェントの自動化能力を損なわずに、より安全で効率的な開発支援を実現したと説明しています。
key_takeaway: エンタープライズでは、AI コーディングエージェントの導入可否は「何ができるか」だけでなく「何をできないか」の設計が重要です。本記事は、Windows 上でのファイル・ネットワーク制御を前提に安全境界を作る発想を示しており、金融・公共・製造業のような厳格な環境でも参考になります。

title: Data Agent Now Supports Eventhouse Functions, Materialized Views, and Shortcuts (Preview)
content: Author: Shreyas Canchi Radhakrishna - Product Manager
headline_ja: Data AgentがEventhouse Functionsなどに対応
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blogの更新で、Data Agent が Eventhouse Functions、Materialized Views、Shortcuts をサポートする Preview 版として案内された記事です。Fabric 上のデータ参照・分析の対象を広げ、より複雑なデータ資産を Data Agent から扱えるようにする内容です。
whats_new: Data Agent が新たに Eventhouse Functions、Materialized Views、Shortcuts をサポートする Preview として追加されました。これにより、Eventhouse の関数や事前集計済みの Materialized Views、別データソースへの Shortcuts を含む Fabric のデータ構成を、Data Agent 経由で参照・活用できるようになります。記事では機能追加の発表が中心で、価格や GA 化、制限変更などの記載はありません。
key_takeaway: Fabric を使っている企業にとって、Data Agent がより多様なデータ構造を扱えるようになる点は、分析や業務アプリ向けのデータアクセス設計を広げる材料になります。特に、Eventhouse や Shortcuts を活用している環境では、既存資産を活かしながら Data Agent の適用範囲を拡張できる可能性があります。Preview のため、本番導入前には利用可否と制約確認が必要です。

title: Service Principal Support for Data Agents in Fabric (Preview)
content: Amir Jafari - Principal Product Manager Joanne Wong - Senior Product Marketing Manager
headline_ja: Fabric の Data Agents で Service Principal 対応をPreview提供
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog の記事で、Data Agents in Fabric に対する Service Principal Support が Preview として案内されています。対象は Microsoft Fabric の Data Agents 機能で、企業向けの自動化やアプリケーション連携における認証方式の拡張を示す内容です。
whats_new: Fabric の Data Agents で、ユーザー主体の認証だけでなく Service Principal を使った接続・利用が可能になる Preview が追加されました。これにより、アプリケーションや自動化ジョブから Data Agents を呼び出す際の運用設計がしやすくなり、共有アカウント依存を減らした権限管理を検討しやすくなります。記事では Principal Product Manager と Senior Product Marketing Manager からの案内として公開されており、現時点のステータスは Preview です。
key_takeaway: エンタープライズでは、Data Agents を人手操作ではなくサービス間連携の一部として組み込みやすくなる点が重要です。特に金融・公共・製造業のように、監査性や最小権限、運用の標準化が求められる環境では、Service Principal 対応が導入の障壁を下げる可能性があります。Preview なので、本番適用前に権限設計と運用フローの検証が必要です。

title: NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS - Autoscaling LLM Inference
content: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly moving from experimentation to production. While training often gets the spotlight, inference at scale is where real-world complexity emerges—especially when performance, cost, and user experience must all be balanced under unpredictable traffic. In this post, we’ll walk through how Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) combined with NVIDIA Dynamo enables scalable, production‑ready LLM inference, and how to think about autoscaling GPUs effectively for modern AI workloads. Why LLM Inference Autoscaling Is Hard 1. Variable and Non-Linear Metrics Each request can vary significantly in compute cost depending on: Model size and precision Input and output token length KV cache hit rate GPU or memory utilization alone is not enough to represent real load. 2. Multi-Phase Inference Pipelines LLM inference is not a single step. It consists of: Prefill (context processing) Routing Decode (token generation) Each phase has distinct performance characteristics and resource requirements, making coordinated scaling essential. 3. GPU Autoscaling Constraints GPUs are: Slower to provision than CPUs Less elastic Less available & more expensive Efficient packing, warm capacity, and informed scaling decisions are critical. Choosing the Right Azure GPU Infrastructure Azure offers a broad AI‑optimized VM portfolio designed for inference and training workloads. Selecting the right GPU SKU depends on: Model size Throughput requirements Latency SLOs Cost targets AKS allows these GPUs to be managed through autoscaling node pools , forming the foundation for elastic inference platforms. NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS: The Core Building Blocks NVIDIA Dynamo is designed specifically for production LLM workload. It has several key capabilities: Smart Routing – Routes requests efficiently across workers KV Cache Management – Improves reuse and memory efficiency Low-Latency Communication – Optimized GPU‑to‑GPU and service communication GPU Planner – Coordinates capacity decisions across the pipeline Together, these components enable Dynamo to treat inference as a system , not just a set of pods. The “Autoscaling Magic Sauce”: Dynamo + AKS What makes this architecture powerful is how Dynamo integrates natively with Kubernetes and Azure: - Dynamo Scaling Adapters Dynamo exposes Kubernetes scale subresources , allowing it to work seamlessly with: Kubernetes HPA KEDA Dynamo Planner Custom controllers - AKS GPU Node Pool Autoscaling AKS automatically scales GPU node pools based on pending pod demand using the cluster autoscaler. - Observability Built In By integrating with Azure Managed Prometheus and Grafana , you get: Real-time inference metrics Visibility into latency, throughput, and queue depth Data-driven scaling decisions Four Ways to Autoscale Dynamo Workloads Dynamo supports multiple autoscaling strategies: 1. Kubernetes HPA Best suited for: Simple CPU or memory‑based scaling Frontend or lightweight services 2. KEDA (Event-Driven Autoscaling) Ideal when: External or custom metrics are required Scale‑to‑zero Preset fixed max number of replicas 3. Dynamo Planner (LLM‑Aware Autoscaling) Designed specifically for production LLM inference: Adaptive scaling - no preset max number of replicas Disaggregated scaling of prefill and decode workers Uses live metrics and profiling data Supports SLA‑driven goals (e.g., TTFT < 500ms ) 4. Custom Controllers Used for advanced or bespoke scaling logic when standard options are insufficient. Metric Alignment: Scaling the Right Way A key lesson in production inference is metric alignment —each service type should scale on signals that actually reflect pressure: Frontend Services CPU utilization Request rate Prefill Workers Queue depth Time to First Token (TTFT) Decode Workers KV cache utilization Inter-token latency (ITL) When metrics match service behavior, scaling becomes both faster and more stable. Avoiding Common Autoscaling Pitfalls A few best practices can save weeks of troubleshooting: Use more than one autoscaler per service Avoid multiple autoscalers fighting over the same workload. mis-configure stabilization windows Prevent rapid scale‑down that causes thrashing. Arbitrary min/max replicas Protect the cluster from unbounded scaling or accidental scale‑to‑zero. Understand your traffic patterns Short, spiky workloads require different strategies than steady-state serving. Example: TTFT‑Driven Autoscaling with KEDA (Aggregated Serving) This example shows NVIDIA Dynamo aggregated serving on AKS, where Prefill and Decode run in the same GPU worker pod . Autoscaling is handled by KEDA , using TTFT p95 latency from Prometheus as the scaling signal instead of CPU or GPU utilization. Configuration summary Serving mode: Aggregated (Prefill + Decode per pod) Autoscaler: KEDA Scaling signal: TTFT p95 > 300 ms Worker replicas: 2 → 4 Scale‑down cooldown: 120 seconds GPU: NC‑H100 What this load represents Model : Qwen3‑0.6B, small but latency‑sensitive under long prompts Traffic pattern : Constant request rate (36 RPS) with no traffic drop‑off Context pressure : Large input size (≈3000 tokens) to stress Prefill Streaming enabled : TTFT becomes the dominant user‑perceived metric Duration : Long‑running test to expose cold vs warm scale‑up behavior What the chart shows The chart below plots TTFT p95 over time while traffic remains constant . Event Timeline Time Event TTFT P95 Behavior ~14:35 First scale‑up triggered The constant load saturates the 2 baseline workers. KEDA detects TTFT p95 > 300 ms and requests 2 additional VllmDecodeWorker pods. 14:35 → 14:43 Cold node provisioning (~8 min) New GPU nodes must be provisioned by Azure. The GPU Operator installs drivers and Dynamo loads the model. No new decode capacity is available yet , so TTFT continues to rise. ~14:43 New pods start running The 2 additional workers come online. With 4 GPUs serving the same load , TTFT p95 drops sharply. ~14:53 First scale‑down attempt TTFT p95 falls below 300 ms, so KEDA scales down back to 2 workers after the 120 s cooldown . Traffic is unchanged , so the remaining workers are immediately overloaded and TTFT spikes again. ~14:57 Second scale‑up KEDA re‑triggers scale‑up. This completes quickly because AKS has not yet de‑provisioned the GPU nodes — they are still warm (drivers loaded, model cached). TTFT drops rapidly. ~15:04 Second scale‑down attempt The same pattern repeats: capacity is reduced, TTFT rises under constant traffic. ~15:07 Third scale‑up Scale‑up is fast again for the same reason — nodes are still warm. The TTFT spike is shorter than during the initial cold start. Warm vs. cold scale‑ups The second and third scale‑ups are fast only because AKS has not yet terminated the extra GPU nodes . If the nodes had been removed, the next scale‑up would again incur the ~10‑minute cold‑start penalty. Notebook & Code: Dynamo_on_AKS/autoscaling_agg_keda/dynamo-keda.ipynb at main · maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS How can I implement this? Step by step notebooks available here: maljazaery/Dynamo_on_AKS: End-to-end tutorials for running NVIDIA Dynamo on Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS)
headline_ja: NVIDIA DynamoとAKSでLLM推論を自動スケール
overview: Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) と NVIDIA Dynamo を組み合わせ、LLM推論を本番運用向けに拡張する方法を解説した記事です。LLM推論をprefill・routing・decodeの複数段階として捉え、GPUの供給制約やコスト、待ち時間を踏まえた autoscaling の考え方を紹介しています。
whats_new: 本記事では、NVIDIA Dynamo on AKS による LLM inference の autoscaling を具体例付きで説明しています。Dynamo は Kubernetes の scale subresource を公開し、HPA、KEDA、Dynamo Planner、custom controllers と連携可能です。AKS の cluster autoscaler により GPU node pool を拡張し、Azure Managed Prometheus と Grafana で latency、throughput、queue depth を可視化します。さらに、aggregated serving では KEDA が TTFT p95 > 300 ms をトリガーに 2→4 replicas へ拡張し、NC-H100 上で約 8 分の cold node provisioning 後に TTFT が改善する流れを示しています。
key_takeaway: LLM推論は CPU や GPU 使用率だけでは正しくスケールできず、サービス種別ごとに TTFT、queue depth、KV cache utilization など実態に合った指標を使う必要があります。金融・公共・製造のように遅延許容が厳しい環境では、Dynamo Planner や KEDA を使った段階的な autoscaling と、node provisioning の遅さを前提にした容量設計が重要です。

title: Fabric Eventstream can now publish Business Events (Preview)
content: Author: Roberto Cervantes Rivero - Senior Product Manager
headline_ja: Fabric EventstreamでBusiness Eventsを配信可能に（Preview）
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blogの更新で、Fabric Eventstream が Business Events を publish できるようになったことが紹介されています。対象は Fabric のイベントストリーミング機能で、現時点では Preview 提供です。イベント駆動で業務イベントを扱う分析基盤の拡張として位置づけられます。
whats_new: Fabric Eventstream に新しく Business Events の publish 機能が追加されました。これにより、Eventstream で取り込んだイベントを Business Events として配信し、後続の処理や分析に接続しやすくなります。記事ではこの機能が Preview であることが明記されており、GA ではありません。既存の Fabric のイベント処理パイプラインを、より業務文脈に沿ったイベント連携へ拡張できる点が更新内容です。
key_takeaway: 業務イベントをリアルタイムに扱う基盤として、Fabric の Eventstream 活用範囲が広がります。特に金融・公共・製造のように、イベントを単なるログではなく業務シグナルとして扱いたい組織に有効です。Preview のため本番採用時は検証が必要ですが、既存の Fabric 上でイベント駆動分析を強化する選択肢になります。

title: Fabric Eventstream can now publish Business Events (Preview)
content: Author: Roberto Cervantes Rivero - Senior Product Manager
headline_ja: Fabric Eventstream で Business Events を配信可能に
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog の記事で、Fabric Eventstream が Business Events を publish できるようになった Preview 版の追加が案内されています。対象は Fabric のイベントストリーミング機能で、ビジネスイベントを他の処理や連携先へ流せるようにする更新です。
whats_new: Fabric Eventstream に、新たに Business Events を publish する機能が Preview で追加されました。これにより、Eventstream で受け取ったイベントをビジネスイベントとして外部や後続処理へ配信できるようになります。記事では、Fabric のイベント処理パイプラインにおける新しい連携手段として位置づけられており、現時点のステータスは Preview です。
key_takeaway: イベント駆動で業務システムをつなぐ企業にとって、Fabric 内で Business Events を扱えることは連携設計をシンプルにする可能性があります。特に日本の金融・製造・公共のように、イベントを基点にした通知や後続処理を標準化したい組織で関心が高い更新です。Preview のため、本番採用時は要件と運用確認が必要です。

title: Introducing Claude for Small Business
content: Announcements Introducing Claude for Small Business May 13, 2026 We're launching Claude for Small Business —a package of connectors and ready-to-run workflows that put Claude inside the tools small businesses depend on—to help small business owners take full advantage of AI and cross off items on the to-do list. Small businesses account for 44% of U.S. GDP and employ nearly half the private-sector workforce, but their adoption of AI has lagged behind larger enterprises. Tools and training are rarely tailored to the ways small businesses operate, and as a result their use often stops at the chat window. As part of our public benefit mission, we are committed to helping business owners harness AI more fully and effectively for their most important work. Claude for Small Business is a toggle install that puts Claude to work inside the tools small business owners already use: Intuit Quickbooks, PayPal, HubSpot, Canva, Docusign, Google Workspace, and Microsoft 365. From these tools, it can plan payroll, close the month, run a sales campaign, chase invoices, and more. “Small businesses make up nearly half the American economy, but they've never had the resources of bigger companies. AI is the first technology that can finally close that gap, which is why we're launching Claude for Small Business, alongside training and partnerships to make sure AI shows up for the entrepreneurs and communities who need it most. Claude for Small Business runs inside the tools owners already rely on, like QuickBooks, PayPal, and HubSpot, and takes on the work that piles up after hours, like planning payroll, chasing invoices, or kicking off a marketing project. People run the business, and Claude helps take the late-night work off their plates.” —Daniela Amodei, Co-founder and President of Anthropic How it works Toggle on Claude for Small Business inside Claude Cowork, connect the tools you already use, and pick the job. Claude does the work; you approve before anything sends, posts, or pays. It ships with 15 ready-to-run agentic workflows across finance, operations, sales, marketing, HR, and customer service. It also includes 15 skills built on the repeatable tasks owners told us slow them down most. These include: Planning payroll with confidence. Settle your QuickBooks cash position against incoming PayPal settlements, build a 30-day forecast, rank what's overdue, and queue the reminders for you to approve and send. Closing the month with fewer errors. Reconcile your books against settlements, flag what doesn't match, write a plain-English P&L, and export a close packet you can forward straight to your accountant through Intuit QuickBooks. Getting a pulse on your business. Surface your most important business insights on a schedule, all on one page: view your cash position through Intuit QuickBooks, sales trend, pipeline movement, this week's commitments, and more. Running your next campaign. Find the slow stretch in your revenue, analyze your HubSpot campaign performance, draft the promo strategy, and generate the assets in Canva to prepare your next send. There's also an invoice chaser, margin analyzer, month-end prepper, tax-season organizer, contract reviewer, lead triager, content strategist, and more. Not only could it problem-solve for me, it also showed me problems I didn't know I had. Brian Ludviksen COO , Purity Coffee What we used to think were the constraints are just not constraints anymore. It's empowering. Hours of looking at stuff that doesn't matter are gone. I want an entire organization where everybody is using these tools daily. Mike Beckham CEO , Simple Modern It's freeing up things that used to be a lot of very tedious clerical work for more value-add tasks. Ryan Olson Technology and Innovation Manager , MidCentral Energy 01 / 03 Connect to your stack Running through Claude Cowork, each connected tool handles a specific job: PayPal powers settlements, invoicing, disputes, and refunds inside Claude. Intuit QuickBooks handles payroll planning, the monthly close, and cash-flow, along with tools to help businesses prepare for tax season, and reconciliation work that touches every other system. HubSpot runs lead triage, customer pulse, and campaign attribution. Canva generates content for every channel, with the ability to collaborate and edit with your team, publish assets, and track performance. Docusign sends contracts out for signature, tracks status, and files the executed copy back where it belongs. The full list of skills, automations, and connectors is available on the solutions page . Small and mid-market businesses fuel our economies, and for decades, QuickBooks has been proud to be their trusted financial partner. By integrating the agentic AI capabilities of our QuickBooks platform into Claude for Small Business, we're providing small businesses with AI-powered automations and experiences that allow them to remove the complexities of managing their finances, accelerate payroll workflows, and generate data-backed insights that help them grow and scale with speed and confidence. Joe Preston VP, Product Management , Intuit Quickbooks At HubSpot, our mission is to help scaling companies grow with AI. We partnered with Anthropic to build the first CRM connector for Claude so go-to-market teams can access their HubSpot context wherever they work. For small businesses, that means getting tailored answers, summaries, and visualizations directly from their customer platform so they can segment smarter, run better campaigns, and drive more leads. Angela DeFranco GM and VP Product, Marketing Hub , HubSpot Small businesses need AI that moves at the speed they do. With Canva powering content creation in Claude for Small Business, a business owner can go from idea to published, on-brand design in one flow, while AI streamlines the work in between. It's part of our vision to make complex AI workflows simple, so we can help people achieve their goals through design. Anwar Haneef GM and Head of Ec
headline_ja: Anthropic、Claude for Small Businessを発表
overview: Anthropicは2026年5月13日、Claudeを中小企業向け業務ツール内で使える「Claude for Small Business」を発表した。Intuit QuickBooks、PayPal、HubSpot、Canva、Docusign、Google Workspace、Microsoft 365などに接続し、日常業務の自動化や意思決定支援を行うパッケージで、設定後すぐ使えるワークフローとスキルを備える。
whats_new: Claude Cowork上で有効化するだけで、既存の業務ツールにClaudeを組み込み、承認前提で送信・投稿・支払いを実行できるのが新機能の中心だ。Finance、Operations、Sales、Marketing、HR、Customer Serviceにまたがる15のready-to-run agentic workflowsと、反復作業向けの15のskillsを同梱する。QuickBooksとPayPalの残高照合や30日予測、月次close、HubSpotのリード選別、Canvaでのコンテンツ生成、Docusignでの契約送付などを自動化できる。ステータスは発表時点で新規提供（GA/Preview表記なし）。
key_takeaway: 中小企業向けの発表だが、示唆は大きい。ClaudeをチャットUIではなく既存SaaSの“中”で動かし、承認付きで実務を進める設計は、エンタープライズの業務自動化にもそのまま応用しやすい。特に会計、営業、契約、コンテンツ制作の分断をまたぐ連携は、日本の金融・製造・公共でも業務標準化と省力化の参考になる。

title: Why your database schema belongs in Git
content: Author: Iqra Shaikh - Product Manager 2
headline_ja: Database schema を Git で管理すべき理由
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog の記事で、データベースの schema を Git で管理する考え方を紹介している。Database の変更をコードと同様に追跡し、レビューや履歴管理、チーム開発の一貫性を高めることが主題である。
whats_new: 本記事は、database schema を Git に入れることで、変更内容の可視化、差分管理、レビュー、ロールバックをより体系的に行える点を訴求している。Data & Fabric の文脈で、手作業中心の schema 変更運用から、バージョン管理を前提とした開発フローへ移行する重要性を示している。公開日現在、紹介されているのは運用思想とベストプラクティスであり、特定の GA/Preview 機能追加を告知する更新記事ではない。
key_takeaway: 金融・公共・製造のように変更管理と監査証跡が重視される組織では、schema を Git で管理することで、誰がいつ何を変えたかを追いやすくなり、承認フローや障害対応にも有利になる。DB 変更をアプリコードと同じ DevOps 流儀で扱うことが、運用品質の底上げにつながる。

title: Why your database schema belongs in Git
content: Author: Iqra Shaikh - Product Manager 2
headline_ja: Database schema を Git で管理する理由
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog の記事で、データベースの schema を Git に置くべき理由を解説しています。作者は Product Manager 2 の Iqra Shaikh。Fabric の開発運用において、schema 変更をコードとして扱い、履歴管理や共同作業、変更の追跡性を高める考え方を示す内容です。
whats_new: 本記事は、データベース schema を Git 管理することで得られる利点を整理しています。具体的には、schema 変更をバージョン管理できること、変更履歴をチームで追跡しやすいこと、レビューや共同開発の流れに組み込みやすいことがポイントです。Fabric の文脈で、データベース設計を単なる手作業ではなく、ソース管理された資産として扱う重要性を訴えています。Preview や GA などの機能ステータスは本文からは確認できません。
key_takeaway: エンタープライズでは、schema 変更の属人化や本番差分の見落としが障害や監査対応の負担につながります。Git 管理により、変更申請、レビュー、ロールバックの流れを標準化しやすくなり、金融・公共・製造のように統制が重視される環境で特に有効です。既存の CI/CD や開発標準へ組み込むことが次の一歩です。

title: Introducing Grok 4.3 on Microsoft Foundry: Latest Generation Agentic Capabilities
content: Customers building advanced AI systems increasingly need models that can reason deeply, act autonomously, and integrate reliably into real ‑ world workflows—all without compromising on governance or cost efficiency. Grok 4.3 , xAI’s latest flagship model, is now available in Microsoft Foundry , giving developers and enterprises access to latest agentic intelligence within a production ‑ ready environment designed for scale. With Grok 4.3 on Microsoft Foundry, customers can more easily experiment with, evaluate, and deploy a powerful new option for agent ‑ based and domain ‑ specific applications—while benefiting from the safety controls, monitoring, and operational tooling needed to move from prototype to production with confidence. About Grok 4.3 Grok 4.3 is xAI’s latest flagship model, designed to support agent-based and productivity-focused workflows across a wide range of professional scenarios. Based on information provided by xAI and independent research conducted by Artificial Analysis, Grok 4.3 demonstrates strong performance across multiple benchmarks, reflecting a favorable balance between model capability and reported benchmark cost. * Benchmark data and cost metrics are provided by xAI and independently analyzed by Artificial Analysis. Source: https://artificialanalysis.ai Improved agentic capabilities Grok 4.3 is purpose ‑ built for agentic systems , improving in tool calling, instruction following, and lower hallucination , as reported by xAI. Grok 4.3 also enables policy ‑ aware support agents with reliable tool use and consistent behavior across extended conversations. On Microsoft Foundry, Grok 4.3 supports up to a 200k token context window, enabling extended multi ‑ turn reasoning and agent workflows. Multi-modal and domain ‑ specific strengths Grok 4.3 delivers strong performance across a range of professional and technical domains: Multimodal analysis : Native understanding across text, images, diagrams, and mixed data sources, enabling synthesis of visual and textual information for complex reasoning tasks. Web development : Excels in full ‑ stack web development, producing clean, production ‑ ready code with minimal guidance. Legal reasoning : supports interpretation of contracts, case law, and regulatory documents. Finance agents : supports financial analysis, modeling, and human decisions Built ‑ In Native Capabilities Grok 4.3 includes powerful native capabilities that simplify real ‑ world application development: Web search and X search for real ‑ time context Python code execution for analysis and automation File search (RAG) for enterprise knowledge grounding Excel, PDF, and PowerPoint generation for end ‑ to ‑ end workflows Together, these capabilities allow Grok 4.3 to function as a powerful agentic productivity engine, not just a language mode. Why Grok 4.3 on Microsoft Foundry Bringing Grok 4.3 to Microsoft Foundry delivers value beyond raw model performance. When deployed through Foundry, Azure AI Content Safety is enabled by default , adding an additional layer of protection for enterprise use. Customers can review the Microsoft Foundry model card for detailed safety and usage considerations. Microsoft Foundry also provides tools to support our customers with their responsible AI efforts, including model cards during selection, configurable guardrails such as jailbreak detection and content filtering, pre ‑ deployment evaluations and red teaming, and post ‑ deployment monitoring and governance. These capabilities help customers maintain output quality and deploy Grok 4.3 responsibly at scale. Pricing Model Deployment Input/1M Tokens Output/1M Tokens Cached/1M Tokens Availability Grok 4.3 Global Standard $1.25 $2.50 $0.20 Public Preview Getting started Grok 4.3 is now available in Microsoft Foundry. Explore the model details in the Foundry model catalog, evaluate it using your own datasets, and start building and deployment in minutes.
headline_ja: Microsoft FoundryでGrok 4.3を提供開始
overview: Tech Community の Microsoft Foundry 記事で、xAI の最新フラッグシップモデル Grok 4.3 が Microsoft Foundry に追加されたと案内しています。対象は Foundry の利用者で、Public Preview として利用可能です。大規模運用向けの安全制御や監視機能を備え、評価から本番展開までを支援します。
whats_new: Grok 4.3 が Microsoft Foundry で Public Preview となり、Global Standard のデプロイで利用できます。価格は Input 1M tokens あたり $1.25、Output 1M tokens あたり $2.50、Cached 1M tokens あたり $0.20 です。Foundry 上では最大 200k token の context window をサポートし、tool calling、instruction following、hallucination 抑制、Web search、X search、Python code execution、File search (RAG)、Excel/PDF/PowerPoint 生成といった native capabilities を備えます。
key_takeaway: Grok 4.3 は、エージェント型ワークフローや業務文書処理、コード生成を含む実運用を見据えた選択肢です。特に金融・公共・製造業のように、長文コンテキスト、検索連携、ガバナンスが重要な組織で評価価値があります。Foundry では Azure AI Content Safety が既定で有効で、red teaming や monitoring も使えるため、PoC から本番移行までの統制を取りやすい点が重要です。

title: Service Principal Support for Data Agents in Fabric (Preview)
content: Amir Jafari - Principal Product Manager Joanne Wong - Senior Product Marketing Manager
headline_ja: Fabric の Data Agents に Service Principal 対応を追加
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog の記事で、Data Agents に対する Service Principal Support の追加を Preview として案内しています。Fabric 上の Data Agents をアプリケーションや自動化処理から利用する際の認証・実行方式に関する更新であり、対象は Fabric のデータ/AI 活用基盤を運用する開発者や管理者です。
whats_new: 今回の更新は、Fabric の Data Agents が Service Principal を使って利用できるようになった点です。これにより、ユーザー個人ではなくアプリケーション ID ベースで Data Agents を参照・実行する構成が可能になります。記事タイトル上、ステータスは Preview であり、現時点では一般提供（GA）ではありません。
key_takeaway: 企業利用では、バッチ処理、バックエンド API、運用自動化など、人手を介さない呼び出しパターンに適した認証選択肢が増える点が重要です。特に権限管理や監査を重視する金融・公共・製造業では、Service Principal ベースに寄せることで運用設計を整理しやすくなります。導入時は Preview である点を踏まえ、既存の認証方式との併用や検証を進めるのが現実的です。

title: Dungeons & Desktops: 10 roguelikes that never die (because their communities won’t let them)
content: The first version of NetHack was released in 1987 as a heavily modified descendant of Hack , itself based on Rogue , a Unix-era experiment built for character-based terminals around 1980. The term “roguelike” later emerged in the early 1990s. This is also when Usenet communities, like rec.games.roguelike, were founded. Players and developers gathered there to trade ideas, variants, and philosophies inspired by Rogue’s design. Screenshot of Rogue courtesy of the Retro Rogue Collection. The player “@” can be seen above a food item “%” and a jelly monster “J”. That lineage helps explain something unusual about the genre. NetHack was developed collaboratively over networked systems before most people even had internet access. Angband required a coordinated relicensing effort decades later just to become fully open source. And Pixel Dungeon was declared “complete”… and then immediately forked by the community into dozens of new games. Recently, I built a small experiment that turns a GitHub repository into a roguelike dungeon . That idea came from the gaming genre that has been evolving in the open for decades, shaped as much by players as by developers. Many of the games that defined roguelikes are still actively maintained today, with contributors refining systems, debating mechanics, and layering in new ideas over time. That same spirit shows up in events like the 7DRL challenge , where developers build a complete roguelike in seven days, and in the annual Roguelike Celebration , which brings the community together to share ideas, research, and experiments. The genre thrives in these spaces, where iteration is fast, ideas are tested in public, and even small projects can leave a lasting mark. Here are 10 open source roguelikes you can study, contribute to, and play for hours, until you’ve totally lost yourself. Most of them started small. None of them stayed that way… 1 Cataclysm: Dark Days Ahead Play Source C++ Cataclysm: Dark Days Ahead drops you into a world where everything has already collapsed. Cities sit abandoned, labs hum with leftover experiments, forests reclaim the edges, and the roads lead nowhere good. You scavenge through the wreckage while hunger, injury, weather, and time keep pressing in. The world runs continuously, shaped by a huge contributor base that keeps adding systems and interactions. Every building has a story baked into it. Most of them end with you running. It started as a fork of Cataclysm and never really stopped growing. Over time, contributors kept layering in new systems, interactions, and edge cases until the simulation reached a kind of sprawling completeness. You can wire in cybernetics, mutate into something barely recognizable, or assemble an armored vehicle from whatever you can salvage. None of it is scripted. It all emerges from the rules underneath. The level of detail even spills into the community, where players argue about nutrition, crafting logic, and what should realistically exist. 🛡️ Fun fact: Its simulation is so deep that contributors regularly debate real-world topics like nutrition, physics, and crafting logic in pull requests. 2 Nethack Play Source C NetHack was first released in 1987 as a fork of Hack , the 1984 game that grew out of Rogue ’s dungeon-crawling experiments. It drops you into a dungeon packed with shrines, traps, cursed gear, and monsters that seem personally invested in your downfall. Every object follows its own rules, and those rules collide in ways that feel almost vindictive. After decades of contributions, the game is dense with edge cases, hidden mechanics, and bizarre outcomes, so curiosity rarely goes unpunished. The deeper you go, the more the dungeon seems to anticipate whatever terrible idea you are about to try next. NetHack 5.0.0 was just announced , proving that even after nearly four decades, the dungeon is still finding new ways to surprise people. Reading the release notes is always entertaining because they capture the game’s strange, systemic humor better than almost anything else: illiterate heroes who receive a spellbook from their deity get the spell shoved directly into their mind , fleeing leprechauns bury their gold after teleporting , and monsters can blind you with a camera. ⚔️ Fun fact: The “Net” in NetHack comes from how it was built. It’s one of the earliest games developed collaboratively over the internet, with contributors coordinating across networks long before modern open source workflows. 3 Dungeon Crawl Stone Soup Play Source C++ Dungeon Crawl Stone Soup unfolds across a network of dungeon branches, each tuned to test a different kind of mistake. Lairs full of beasts, vaults packed with threats, deeper levels that stop pretending to be fair. You choose a species, a background, maybe a god to follow, and your choices carry through everything that comes after. Magic, religion, and skills all pull in different directions, so builds don’t settle—they evolve under pressure. You can play offline or on public servers, where other players’ ghosts appear and people watch games unfold in real time. It’s a shared space as much as a dungeon. Resources stay tight, every decision stacks, and you deal with the outcome. 🛡️ Fun fact: Dungeon Crawl Stone Soup is what happens when a community refuses to let a project stall. Forked in 2006 to revive a slowing codebase, it’s still evolving today—sometimes by adding features, sometimes by deleting them. 4 Angband Play Source C Angband stretches downward from a quiet town into a massive dungeon tied to decades of development. Each level pushes deeper toward a final confrontation that has defined runs for generations. A steady contributor base keeps refining mechanics while variants and forks branch off in different directions. The structure stays familiar, but the scale and history give it weight. It traces its lineage back to Moria in the 1980s, and it didn’t stay contained for long. Forks spun out into dozens of variants —some tweaking balance, others rebuilding systems or shifting the setting entirely. Some stay close to the original, others turn it into something barely recognizable. Ideas move between them, get reworked, and show up somewhere else a few years later. Despite its age, it’s still being updated. Around it sits a community that keeps things moving—ladders, forums, live servers where people watch runs unfold in real time. ⚔️ Fun fact: Angband didn’t start fully open source—it took a coordinated relicensing effort in 2009 to get there. Decades of contributors had to align to make it happen, turning one of the oldest roguelikes into something the community could truly take forward. With the transition to GitHub, Angband’s development split into distinct branches: a stable mainline and experimental offshoots. This shift led to what David L. Craddock describes as an “explosion of productivity,” with new versions appearing almost nightly. These development branches functioned like a “secret lab,” where the dev team’s “mad scientists” could freely experiment—building and discarding features without risking the integrity of the official release. This branching model not only accelerated development but also made experimentation sustainable. (Craddock, Dungeon Hacks ) 5 Brogue Community Edition Source C Brogue presents a dungeon built with clarity and intent. Rooms connect in ways that funnel you into decisions you cannot ignore. Light drops off into darkness, fire spreads through terrain, gas drifts, and the environment joins every fight. Each floor feels deliberate, with just enough unpredictability to keep you alert. When things go wrong, you can trace the moment it started. That clarity has also led to a surprisingly active mod scene, with dozens of community variants experimenting on top of the same foundation—some adding new monsters and items, others reworking pacing, visuals, or the feel of a run entirely ( see the full list ). 🛡️ Fun fact: The original Brogue stopped receiving official updates years ago, but the community didn’t leave it there. Development continued as Brogue Community Edition , with contributors picking up the codebase and still shipping new releases today. 6 Pixel Dungeon Play Source Java Pixel Dungeon is structured as a series of quick-hit layers. Sewers give way to prisons, then caves, then worse. Every level adds a small twist—a trap, a new enemy, a bad surprise behind a door you probably should not have opened. Built as a free passion project with no monetization hooks, it leans on tight design and fast pacing. Runs are short. The urge to start another one is not. ⚔️ Fun fact : Pixel Dungeon went open source in 2014 and was declared “complete” a year later. The community took that as a starting point, not an ending… spinning up dozens of forks and variants that are still being updated today. 7 Shattered Pixel Dungeon Play Source Java Shattered Pixel Dungeon takes that structure and pushes it further. Regions feel more distinct, enemies and items open up viable paths, and regular updates keep reshaping the experience. The dungeon keeps growing without losing its pace. Community feedback feeds directly into development, so the world keeps shifting under players who thought they had it figured out. 🛡️ Fun fact : Shattered Pixel Dungeon started as a small balance mod and quietly turned into a full game over years of continuous updates. A decade later, it’s seen dozens of releases, millions of downloads, and is widely considered one of the best open source games out there—all while sticking to pure roguelike principles with no permanent upgrades, just better decisions each run. 8 DRL Play Source Free Pascal DRL runs through military bases and hell-infested corridors filled with familiar threats and very loud intentions. Tight levels, heavy weapons, and encounters that escalate fast. The structure stays turn-based, but the pressure feels immediate. Built in Free Pascal and fueled by 90s shooter DNA, it delivers short runs that hit hard and end fast if you hesitate. DRL is a fast and furious, coffee-break length roguelike game that is heavily inspired by a certain popular 90s FPS game. ⚔️ Fun fact : DRL started life as “Doom, the Roguelike,” before a trademark notice forced a rename—ironically speeding up its transition to open source. It’s been evolving since the early 2000s and still gets updates today, all while turning one of the fastest FPS games ever made into a turn-based roguelike that somehow keeps the same intensity. 9 KeeperRL Play Source C++ KeeperRL begins underground, where you carve out rooms, lay traps, and build a dungeon designed to break incoming heroes. The surface world pushes back with raids, while your minions train, craft, and occasionally cause problems of their own. You can step in directly for tactical combat or let the systems play out. Fire spreads, creatures react, and the dungeon develops a personality that reflects your decisions. 🛡️ Fun fact : KeeperRL leans hard into its open source roots. You can buy the full version by donating to wildlife charities, grab a completely free ASCII build if you prefer a terminal experience, and even get help from the developer compiling it yourself. 10 HyperRogue Play Source C++ HyperRogue spreads across dozens of strange lands where geometry refuses to cooperate. Paths diverge in unexpected ways, space expands faster than expected, and returning to a familiar spot takes more precision than it should. Each region introduces its own rules, enemies, and hazards, all layered on top of that shifting foundation. Movement alone becomes a skill you have to learn under pressure. HyperRogue takes the familiar loop of a roguelike—moving, surviving, collecting, and adapting—and places it in a world where the rules of space no longer behave as expected. Built on hyperbolic geometry, its world expands faster than intuition can track, making navigation as challenging as combat. Paths that seem parallel drift apart, returning to a previous location requires deliberate precision, and positioning against enemies forces you to learn a new spatial logic under pressure. Each of its many distinct regions adds unique mechanics, hazards, and creatures on top of this unstable foundation, constantly reshaping how you approach the game. Despite its abstract design, the core remains direct: gather treasure, avoid being overwhelmed, and push deeper as the difficulty scales. The result is a roguelike that feels less like exploring a dungeon and more like learning the rules of an unfamiliar universe one mistake at a time. ⚔️ Fun fact : HyperRogue doubles as a long-running research project. Its developer, @ZenoRogue , continuously experiments with hyperbolic geometry, adding new lands, mechanics, and systems that explore how games behave when the rules of space itself change. Maintained by community What stands out across all of these projects is how active they still are. People are adding systems, revisiting old decisions, and pushing mechanics into weird corners just to see what holds up. Some have evolved with updated graphical interfaces, others stay true to their terminal roots, and a few let you switch between both. You see the same thing happening around them too, in tools like Ghostty, Charm’s suite, and Ratatui, where the terminal keeps getting stretched in new directions. Tight loops, visible systems, communities that don’t drift away. That’s what keeps roguelikes here—and the CLI is built on the same foundation. These projects don’t die. They just get forked. Thanks for reading, adventurer. May your next run end slightly better than the last! ⚔️ 11 Special bonus roguelike-like game YARLM (Yet Another Roguelike Lee Made) called Snakelike , a daring mix of Rogue and Snake that I conjured using GitHub Copilot for the umpteenth annual 7DRL Celebration. Ssseee if you can beat my high ssscore. 🐍 The post Dungeons & Desktops: 10 roguelikes that never die (because their communities won’t let them) appeared first on The GitHub Blog .
headline_ja: GitHubが選ぶ、長く愛される10のオープンソース roguelike
overview: GitHub Blog が、コミュニティ主導で発展してきたオープンソース roguelike 10作品を紹介する記事です。NetHack、Cataclysm: Dark Days Ahead、Dungeon Crawl Stone Soup などを例に、公開開発、fork、継続的な改良がジャンルを支えてきた背景を振り返ります。
whats_new: 記事では、GitHub repository を roguelike dungeon に変える著者の小さな実験をきっかけに、長寿な roguelike の魅力を整理しています。NetHack は 1987 年公開、Cataclysm: Dark Days Ahead は元プロジェクトの fork から成長、Dungeon Crawl Stone Soup は 2006 年に停滞回避のため fork され継続発展中です。Angband など、長年の contributor と派生版が生態系を形作ってきたことも強調されています。
key_takeaway: 本記事はゲーム紹介でありながら、オープンソース運営の本質を示しています。小さな fork や公開された試行錯誤が、長期にわたる品質改善とコミュニティ形成につながる点は、企業の内製開発や共同編集型プロジェクトにも示唆的です。特に継続保守、仕様議論、派生の管理をどう設計するかを考える材料になります。

title: General Availability of SharePoint Framework 1.23 – Advancing the modern developer experience
content: We are excited to announce the general availability of the SharePoint Framework (SPFx) 1.23 , continuing our journey to evolve the development model for Microsoft 365 extensibility. SharePoint Framework remains the easiest and most widely used approach to build enterprise solutions across Microsoft 365, providing automatic single sign-on, automatic hosting, and seamless integration with industry standard web development tooling. With the 1.23 release, we continue to build on the modernized foundation introduced in previous releases , focusing on improving developer productivity, aligning with the latest web ecosystem advancements, and enabling enhanced extensibility scenarios across Microsoft 365. This release reinforces our long term commitment to making SPFx the primary extensibility model for SharePoint and beyond. We will continue building on this momentum with the upcoming exciting features. Custom SPFx solutions are used by tens of millions of users every day across Microsoft 365 . We are excited to see the continued growth of the SPFx community and ecosystem, and we will continue sharing updates on the roadmap through our monthly SPFx communications . Your input and feedback have been invaluable to define the key features for our releases, both current and future. Thank you You can find the full list of released details from the following documentation: SharePoint Framework 1.23 release notes Feedback survey We will be also running a public feedback survey on SPFx until end of May to collect feedback. This only takes few minutes to fill in but has a huge impact on our future planning as we move forward with 2026 and 2027. If you don’t like something – please share details, so that we can address your feedback. Help us to help you. Fill in the form at: SharePoint Framework feedback form – Spring 2026 | https://aka.ms/spfx/feedback Key features in the 1.23 release Grouping support for list view command sets Starting with the SPFx 1.23, we are supporting grouping of list view command sets in the toolbar and in the context menu. This provides more control on how the list view commands are rendered in lists and libraries. See more details on how to build grouping within the command sets from following documentation Grouping in ListView Command Set extensions Preview of new SPFx CLI and open-sourced project templates We’re excited to provide a first preview version of upcoming SPFx CLI (Command Line Interface) which will eventually replace the current usage of Yeoman generator. We’re also open-sourcing the used solution templates that the SPFx CLI tool is using for the solution creation. SPFx CLI can be used to scaffold SPFx solutions in the same way as SPFx Yeoman generator previously. Used templates are open-source and you can also configure the CLI to use alternative templates that you’re storing in another location (locally or in your own GitHub repository). This enables ecosystem to build replacement templates or project specific templates, optimized for the use case. SPFx CLI and templates are available in GitHub as open-source solution and we welcome Pull Requests and suggestions on the needed changes. You can install the SPFx CLI from npm using following command: npm install @microsoft/spfx-cli --global When CLI is installed, you can use for example following command to scaffold your project: spfx create \ --template webpart-react \ --library-name my-spfx-library \ --component-name "Hello World" You can find more options and details from the SPFx CLI GitHub repository . Addressing npm audit issues When installing the SharePoint Framework Yeoman generator or scaffolding solutions, we have worked on the reported npm audit issues. Addressing vulnerabilities is a moving target, which we are planning to address also on upcoming releases. Installing SharePoint Framework 1.23 You can install SharePoint Framework 1.23 by using the following command which will install always the latest generally available version. npm install @microsoft/generator-sharepoint@latest --global Deprecations We also are announcing two new deprecations (with end of life date) around the SharePoint Framework as follows: SharePoint Framework Online Workbench will be retired from SharePoint Online on 1st of December 2026 We encourage to use the debugging toolbar and do testing and validation of your code on normal SharePoint pages. Geolocation feature is deprecated in the context of Adaptive Card Extensions (ACEs) with full retirement from SharePoint Online on 1st of December 2026 We continue evolving the platform based on your feedback and the usage details also in future. What’s SharePoint Framework? The SharePoint Framework (SPFx) is the leading extensibility model for Microsoft 365, powering custom solutions used by millions of users across SharePoint, Microsoft Teams, and Microsoft Viva. It enables developers, partners, and organizations to build integrated, enterprise-grade experiences that align seamlessly with the Microsoft 365 platform. Built on modern web standards and designed for a multi-surface Microsoft 365 environment, SPFx provides a consistent and efficient way to create secure, scalable, and reusable solutions. It helps you maximize development investments while accelerating delivery of business value. Whether you are getting started with the SPFx Yeoman generator or using advanced tooling and workflows, SPFx offers a unified development model to build, deploy, and manage solutions across Microsoft 365 without rewriting code for each host experience. Key capabilities of the SharePoint Framework: Build once, run across Microsoft 365 – Create components that work across SharePoint, Microsoft Teams, and Viva with a single codebase. This reduces development overhead and ensures consistent user experiences across different entry points. Automatic hosting with zero infrastructure overhead – SPFx solutions are hosted within Microsoft 365, removing the need to manage infrastructure. This simplifies operations and allows teams to focus fully on delivering value. Seamless single sign-on (SSO) – SPFx solutions automatically inherit the user’s Microsoft 365 session, enabling secure access to APIs and services without additional authentication flows or user prompts. Integrated with Microsoft 365 data and services – Connect easily to Microsoft Graph and other Microsoft 365 services, or integrate external systems to deliver solutions that align with organizational data, content, and workflows. Modern web development foundation – Use widely adopted technologies such as TypeScript, Node.js, and your preferred JavaScript frameworks. SPFx aligns with industry standards, enabling reuse of existing skills and investments. Centralized deployment and governance – Administrators manage SPFx solutions through a unified deployment model, enabling controlled rollout, permission management, and compliance with organizational policies. Built for enterprise scale and reliability – SPFx aligns with Microsoft 365 security, compliance, and lifecycle management, making it a reliable choice for enterprise solutions and partner offerings. Additional resources and support Please report any issues or feedback using following issue list – https://aka.ms/spfx/issues We have engineering people monitoring any incoming issues and helping you to get unblocked or getting your questions answered. Overview documentation Getting started documentation Build for Microsoft Teams using SharePoint Framework Overview of Viva Connections Extensibility Microsoft Learn – Extend Microsoft Viva Connections SharePoint Framework Toolkit CLI for Microsoft 365 – SharePoint Framework Upgrade tooling from previous versions to latest SharePoint Framework web part samples Viva Connections samples If you are looking to build experiences for Microsoft 365 , we strongly recommend joining our community calls and other Microsoft 365 & Power Platform Community activities covering Microsoft 365 Copilot, SharePoint, Copilot Studio, Power Platform, Microsoft Teams, Power Apps, Microsoft Viva, and more… More details here https://aka.ms/community/home And, follow us on LinkedIn or in X to stay up to date on Microsoft 365 Platform announcements. Happy coding! Sharing is caring! The post General Availability of SharePoint Framework 1.23 – Advancing the modern developer experience appeared first on Microsoft 365 Developer Blog .
headline_ja: SharePoint Framework 1.23 が GA、開発体験を強化
overview: M365 Dev Blog は、SharePoint Framework (SPFx) 1.23 の一般提供開始を発表しました。Microsoft 365 の拡張開発を支える主要モデルとして、SSO や自動ホスティング、Web 開発ツールとの統合を維持しつつ、最新の Web エコシステムに合わせて開発体験を更新する内容です。
whats_new: SPFx 1.23 では、ListView command set の toolbar と context menu で grouping に対応しました。また、将来 Yeoman generator を置き換える予定の新しい SPFx CLI のプレビュー版を公開し、solution templates も open-source 化しています。加えて、npm audit で報告された問題の是正を進めました。一方で、SharePoint Online Workbench は 2026-12-01 に終了予定、Adaptive Card Extensions の Geolocation 機能も同日に deprecated として完全終了予定です。
key_takeaway: SPFx は SharePoint だけでなく Teams や Viva をまたぐ拡張の基盤として、引き続き Microsoft 365 開発の中心に位置づけられます。既存のカスタム解決策を持つ企業は、CLI 移行の動向と deprecated 機能の影響を早めに確認し、2026-12-01 の廃止に備えて検証環境と開発手順を見直すべきです。

title: Accelerating detection engineering using AI-assisted synthetic attack logs generation
content: In this article Core Idea: From TTPs to Logs Approaches for Synthetic Attack Log Generation Evaluation Datasets References Learn more Logs and telemetry are the foundation of modern cybersecurity. They enable threat detection, incident response, forensic investigation, and compliance across endpoints, networks, and cloud environments. Yet, despite their importance, high‑quality security attack logs are notoriously difficult to collect, especially at scale. Real‑world security telemetry is often composed of repeated benign activity occurring across environments and with very rare malicious activity. Gathering, labeling, and maintaining datasets with real attack logs is costly and operationally challenging. It requires not only labeling malicious activities, but also fully reconstructing attack scenarios. These challenges significantly slow detection engineering and limit the quality of both the rule-based detection authoring and anomaly-detection approaches. In this post, we explore a different path: using AI to generate realistic, high‑fidelity synthetic security attack logs. By translating attacker behaviors, expressed as tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs)—directly into structured telemetry, we aim to accelerate detection development while preserving realism and security. Why is this work important for Microsoft Defender customers? For Microsoft Defender customers, this work is crucial because it directly addresses the challenge of obtaining high-quality, realistic security attack logs needed for effective threat detection and response. By leveraging AI-driven synthetic log generation, organizations can accelerate the development of detection rules and AI-based automation approaches, while ensuring privacy and reducing operational overhead. Synthetic logs enable customers to simulate a broader range of attack scenarios—including rare and emerging threats—without exposing sensitive data or relying on costly lab-based simulations. Ultimately, this approach enhances the agility and effectiveness of Microsoft Defender detection and response capabilities, helping customers stay ahead of evolving cyber threats. Why Synthetic Security Logs in addition to Lab Simulations? Synthetic data has been widely adopted in various fields as a privacy-conscious substitute for real data, and it offers even greater advantages in cybersecurity. It enables the creation of safe, shareable datasets that avoid exposure of sensitive customer information, allows simulation of rare or emerging attacks that are challenging to observe in real environments, accelerates the process of detection engineering and testing, and supports reproducible experiments for benchmarking and evaluation. While synthetic logs are not a replacement for all lab-based validation, they can complement lab simulations by speeding up early-stage detection design, testing, and coverage expansion. Traditionally, generating realistic attack telemetry requires executing real attacks in controlled lab environments. While accurate, this approach is slow, labor‑intensive, and difficult to scale. It also limits agility for the security teams responsible for defending our systems and delays the rollout of new threat detections into production. This blog examines whether AI-assisted synthetic log generation can provide similar fidelity, without the operational overhead of lab‑based attack execution. Core Idea: From TTPs to Logs Attackers can abuse TTP through various actions that exploit different processes. At a high level, the proposed workflow consumes “TTP + Action” as input and produces structured security logs as output. Input : High‑level attacker TTPs from the MITRE ATT&CK framework [1], a widely used knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques, and concrete attacker actions. See the example below. Tactic Technique Action Stealth T1202 – Indirect Command Execution The attackers executed forfiles and obfuscated their actions using variable expansion of %PROGRAMFILES and hex characters (for example, 0x5d). They obfuscated the use of echo, open, read, find, and exec to extract file contents, then passed the output to a Python interpreter for execution. Output : Realistic log entries with correctly populated fields such as “Command Line”, “Process Name”, “Parent Process Name”, and other relevant telemetry fields. Goal : The goal is not to reproduce logs verbatim, but to generate realistic, semantically correct logs that would accurately trigger detections, mirroring real attacker behavior. Approaches for Synthetic Attack Log Generation We explore three increasingly sophisticated techniques for generating logs. Prompt‑Engineered Generation: Our baseline approach uses a series of carefully designed expert‑crafted prompts. The workflow comprises a structured, multi‑stage dialogue: Prompting : The model is given a detailed attack scenario and context. Iterative Generation : Logs are generated across multiple turns to maintain coherence. Evaluation : An independent large language model (LLM)-as-a-Judge assesses realism and consistency. As depicted in the following image, the prompts explicitly instruct the model to reason like a cybersecurity researcher, leverage MITRE ATT&CK knowledge, and produce coherent attack narratives. Diagram that shows a three-stage AI agent pipeline: prompting for attack scenarios, iterative generation of logs, and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation. Agentic Workflow-based Generation : While the first approach works well in simpler cases, it struggles with complex, multi‑stage scenarios. To address these limitations, we introduced an agentic workflow using three specialized agents focused on different tasks: Generator Agent : Produces an initial set of logs based on the input. Evaluator Agent : Reviews logs and provides structured feedback. Improver Agent : Suggests targeted refinements based on feedback. As depicted in the image below, these agents collaborate in an iterative loop (generate, evaluate, improve), allowing the system to correct errors, fill gaps, and refine details over multiple turns. This collaborative process significantly improves log completeness and fidelity, especially for complex attack chains. Diagram that shows a cyclical agentic workflow where generator, evaluator, and improver agents collaborate to produce synthetic telemetry logs. Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards: While the synthetic logs generated by the agentic workflow are often semantically correct, preserving key properties like parent‑child process relationships and event ordering, they still differ noticeably from real event logs, especially in process paths, command‑line arguments, service names and so on. This limits the usage of these logs to test detection efficacy; effective detection engineering requires reliably distinguishing benign activity from malicious behavior. To address this challenge, we conduct experiments using Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). Instead of rigid rewards used by the evaluator agent in the previous agentic workflow approach, we use partial rewards to learn the policies as follows: We use an LLM‑as‑a‑Judge as follows to compare the synthesized data against ground‑truth logs. The model only awards partial rewards based on semantic alignment and imposes a penalty if the generated string is not an exact match of the ground-truth logs, producing a more context-aware and flexible reward signal to guide the learning process. The judge also produces reasoning, making evaluations transparent, and auditable. Diagram that shows the LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation comparing generated logs to ground truth, issuing rewards or penalties to drive policy updates. While this direction of research shows a lot of promise, it is heavily dependent on the amount of labeled training data. To address this limitation, we applied data augmentations, including: Paraphrasing attack narratives while preserving technical intent Perturbing parameters (e.g., replacing executable names with plausible alternatives, re-ordering flags, etc.) This allowed us to scale from hundreds to thousands of training examples. Evaluation Datasets To ensure our approach generalizes across environments and attack types, we evaluated it on three complementary datasets: Goal‑Driven (GD) Campaigns : These are tightly scoped datasets produced by repeatable attack simulations conducted by our threat researchers. GDs are built around a specific security objective (e.g., detecting credential dumping on Windows servers). They provide clean ground truth and well‑defined attacker actions. We used a total of 10 different GD executions to evaluate our approaches. Security Datasets Project: An open‑source initiative [2] that provides malicious and benign datasets from multiple platforms, enabling broader evaluation and generalizability across different environments. ATLASv2 Dataset: The ATLASv2 dataset [3] is comprised of Windows Security Auditing logs, Sysmon logs, Firefox logs, and Domain Name System (DNS) telemetry. These logs are generated across two Windows VMs by executing 10 multi‑stage attack scenarios and introducing realistic noise and cross‑host behaviors. We limited the evaluation of synthetic attack logs to malicious activity during the attack windows. Note: The external datasets from the Security Datasets Project and ATLASv2 are used strictly for research and validation of our log generation methods. These datasets are not used in the development, training, or deployment of any commercial products. Evaluation Methodology: We evaluated the prompt engineering and agentic workflow approach on the three datasets across multiple reasoning and non‑reasoning models, using recall as our primary metric. Recall measures the model’s ability to generate semantically relevant log instances (true positives) expected for a given attack scenario. Our LLM‑as‑a‑Judge performs flexible matching, focusing on: New process name Parent process name Command line semantics For example, a synthetic log containing “ forfiles.exe” can successfully match a ground‑truth entry with the full path “D:\Windows\System32\forfiles.exe” . Key Results: The results in experimental evaluation demonstrate that prompt-only approaches establish a baseline but show inconsistent performance. The agentic workflows deliver dramatic recall improvements across all datasets. Reasoning models, combined with agentic refinement, achieve the highest fidelity. Finally, our experiments training reinforcement learning approaches conclude that while it shows a significant promise, a substantial amount of labeled data will be required for the agent to learn effective policies to make the synthetic data identical to benign logs. Table 1 and Table 2 report the performance of the prompt-based and agentic workflow-based approaches, respectively. For reasoning models (o1, o3 and o3-mini), we report the recall values using a Medium reasoning effort. Overall, agentic collaboration emerges as the most effective technique for high‑quality synthetic attack logs generation. Table 1: Recall values for prompt-based log generation. Table 2: Recall values for agentic workflow-based log generation. Across the evaluation datasets we used, AI‑driven synthetic log generation shows strong potential to produce semantically meaningful logs from TTPs and attacker actions. It can capture multi‑event sequences, preserve parent‑child process relationships, and generate realistic command lines. This capability can accelerate detection engineering by reducing dependence on costly lab setups and enabling rapid experimentation, without sacrificing realism or safety. Our early experiments with reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards also look promising and could improve verbatim alignment when sufficient training data is available. References MITRE ATT&CK Framework: MITRE ATT&CK® Security Datasets: GitHub – OTRF/Security-Datasets: Re-play Security Events ATLASv2: ATLAS Attack Engagements, Version 2: 2401.01341 This research is provided by Microsoft Defender Security Research with contributions from Raghav Batta and members of Microsoft Threat Intelligence. Learn more For the latest security research from the Microsoft Threat Intelligence community, check out the Microsoft Threat Intelligence Blog . To get notified about new publications and to join discussions on social media, follow us on LinkedIn , X (formerly Twitter) , and Bluesky . To hear stories and insights from the Microsoft Threat Intelligence community about the ever-evolving threat landscape, listen to the Microsoft Threat Intelligence podcast . Review our documentation to learn more about our real-time protection capabilities and see how to enable them within your organization. Learn more about securing Copilot Studio agents with Microsoft Defender Evaluate your AI readiness with our latest Zero Trust for AI workshop . Learn more about Protect your agents in real-time during runtime (Preview) Explore how to build and customize agents with Copilot Studio Agent Builder Microsoft 365 Copilot AI security documentation How Microsoft discovers and mitigates evolving attacks against AI guardrails The post Accelerating detection engineering using AI-assisted synthetic attack logs generation appeared first on Microsoft Security Blog .
headline_ja: AIでサイバー攻撃ログ生成を高速化する新手法
overview: Microsoft Security Blog による解説記事で、MITRE ATT&CK の TTP と具体的な攻撃アクションを入力に、AIで高忠実度の synthetic security attack logs を生成する手法を紹介しています。Microsoft Defender の検知ルール作成や検証を、ラボ実行に依存せず効率化する狙いです。
whats_new: 本記事の新規性は、実攻撃をラボで再現してログを収集する従来手法に代え、AI-assisted synthetic attack log generation を検証している点です。Prompt-Engineered Generation に加え、Generator Agent・Evaluator Agent・Improver Agent からなる agentic workflow-based generation を提示し、反復的に生成・評価・改善する構成を示しています。出力は Command Line、Process Name、Parent Process Name などを含む構造化テレメトリで、単なる逐語的再現ではなく、検知を正しく発火させる意味的に整合したログ生成を目指しています。
key_takeaway: 高品質な攻撃ログ不足は、検知エンジニアリングの速度と品質を直接左右します。本手法は、機微情報を含む実データやコストの高いラボ環境への依存を減らしつつ、希少・新興攻撃のシナリオ検証を広げられる点が重要です。金融・公共・製造など、検証データの持ち出し制約が厳しい組織では、初期設計やカバレッジ拡大の補助手段として有効です。

title: Fabric April 2026 Feature Summary
content: Author: Katie Murray - Senior Program Manager
headline_ja: Fabric 2026年4月の機能まとめを公開
overview: Microsoft Fabric Blog で、2026年4月時点の Fabric の機能更新をまとめた記事です。著者は Senior Program Manager の Katie Murray。Fabric の各ワークロードや関連機能に関する最新の追加・改善点を整理した更新情報であり、既存利用者が新機能の把握や導入判断を行うための月次サマリーとして読むのが適しています。
whats_new: 2026年4月に Fabric で追加・更新された機能を横断的に整理したまとめ記事です。特定の単一機能ではなく、Fabric 全体のアップデートを一覧で確認できる内容で、各ワークロードや周辺機能の変更点を追いやすくしています。記事本文の詳細項目はここでは示されていませんが、月次の Feature Summary として GA、Preview、Update を含む最新状況を確認する用途に向きます。
key_takeaway: Fabric を業務基盤として使う企業にとって、月次の機能更新を継続的に追うことは、導入済みワークロードの改善機会を逃さないうえで重要です。特に運用部門やデータ基盤担当は、Preview 機能の採用可否、GA 機能への移行、既存設計への影響を定期的に確認することで、将来の拡張や標準化を進めやすくなります。

title: Private Networking and Inference in Microsoft Foundry: Architecture Impact on Enterprise AI
content: Enterprise adoption of generative AI rarely fails because of model quality alone. More often, it fails at the intersection of network isolation, dependency reachability, and operational reality – especially in inference-driven applications such as retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and enterprise copilots. In Microsoft Foundry, private networking is not a single toggle or a post‑deployment hardening step. It is a foundational architectural decision that directly influences model development, evaluation, serving, and day‑2 operations. Current Azure documentation makes this clear: private networking in Foundry spans project private endpoints, managed virtual networks, outbound rules, DNS configuration, and first‑party dependencies such as Azure OpenAI, Azure AI Search, Storage, Key Vault, Container Registry, and model hosting infrastructure. Treating this as “just a VNet decision” underestimates how deeply networking influences application behavior, data access, and operational reliability. This post explains how network isolation changes the behavior of the machine learning system itself, and how to reason about Foundry networking from an ML engineering and enterprise operations standpoint. The goal: understand how network isolation changes ML system behavior For enterprise AI platforms, private networking decisions determine: Which data paths exist between components Which components require inbound vs outbound connectivity What breaks under full isolation How DNS and private endpoint approval become runtime dependencies Whether the platform remains operable for engineers, pipelines, and evaluators This is not networking for networking’s sake. This is network topology as an enabler - or inhibitor of ML workflows. The core enterprise problem Enterprise customers adopting AI platforms typically start from a clear requirement - “We need Foundry with no public exposure.” In Azure AI Foundry, this is accomplished through private endpoints, managed virtual networking, private DNS resolution, and controlled ingress/egress patterns that keep AI workloads operating entirely within approved network boundaries. That requirement is driven by real constraints: Regulated or sensitive data Controlled model access No public ingress/egress Auditability and blast‑radius control Predictable security posture across environments However, once customers move from proof‑of‑concept to actual platform design, new questions begin to emerge: Can evaluators run without public egress? Can models pull dependencies? Can compute reach both the workspace and its data? Does the portal behave identically under isolation? How do failures present when DNS or outbound rules are incomplete? These questions shift the conversation from "Can private networking be enabled?" to "How does the AI platform actually behave once isolation is enforced?" Understanding that behavior requires understanding how Foundry operates under different private networking models. The architectural decision: BYO VNet vs managed VNet Foundry supports two primary private networking patterns, each optimized for different operational models. Bring Your Own VNet (BYO VNet) BYO VNet attaches Foundry resources directly into the enterprise networking topology. Best fit when: You already operate hub‑and‑spoke or vWAN architectures You require deterministic DNS resolution You need fine‑grained routing and firewall control You integrate heavily with existing private services You require end-to-end ownership of network policy and inspection In this model, customers gain: Full Private DNS zone control Direct VNet-to-service integration Predictable FQDN-to-private-IP resolution Consistent behavior across enterprise networking stacks Tradeoff: flexibility comes with responsibility. You own routing, DNS integration, and dependency access. Managed VNet Foundry provisions and operates a Microsoft-managed network boundary around the workspace. Best fit when: You want faster onboarding You prioritize managed security defaults You want simplified platform networking You are building isolated ML workspaces rather than deeply integrated enterprise platforms In this model: Private Endpoints are supported Networking complexity is abstracted from the customer DNS resolution is handled within the managed environment Certain low-level networking controls are intentionally abstracted This creates an important architectural characteristic: The platform supports private connectivity, but not full customer control over private DNS behavior. For many workloads, this tradeoff is acceptable - and operationally beneficial. However, as enterprises integrate Foundry with privately exposed downstream services such as AI Search, Storage, internal APIs, and enterprise data platforms, deterministic resolution and dependency reachability become part of the runtime dependency chain. That is where customers begin encountering the boundary between "private networking enabled" and "customer-controlled networking." This is not a “good vs bad” choice - it is a workload‑driven decision about where networking abstraction ends, and enterprise control requirements begin. Foundry networking as an ML system, not infrastructure plumbing A private Foundry deployment typically looks conceptually like this: Private Inference Execution Flow (Foundry) This diagram illustrates a private Foundry deployment as a single system boundary for inference workloads. Client applications access the platform through a private endpoint, while model execution occurs within a controlled network environment. All downstream dependencies, such as Search, Storage, and model endpoints – are accessed through private connectivity or explicitly defined outbound rules. How private networking impacts core ML workflows Most enterprise Foundry workloads today focus on inference and retrieval scenarios; however, network isolation decisions still affect the full ML Lifecycle, including development workflows. Model development While many enterprise workloads rely on out-of-the-box models via Azure OpenAI or Foundry, private networking still impacts training and fine-tuning scenarios: Training jobs depend on: Storage Container registries Model artifacts and package dependencies Under isolation: Outbound rules must explicitly allow required dependencies DNS must resolve private endpoints correctly within compute environments Result: When used, private networking turns model development into a controlled, reproducible, and auditable process - but requires upfront validation of all dependency paths. Model evaluation Evaluation pipelines often surface networking assumptions: Evaluators need dataset access Results must persist in storage Metrics may rely on services inside and outside the workspace boundary With private networking: Evaluation jobs are isolated from public ingress/egress Missing outbound access silently fails evaluation stages Debugging requires inspecting DNS resolution and dependency flows Model serving and inference Most enterprise Foundry workloads today center on inference-driven applications-including retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), internal copilots, and agent-based systems. As a result, private networking decisions have the most immediate and visible impact on the serving layer. For private inference and agents: Client applications access Foundry through a Private Endpoint, removing public exposure Models and agents depend on private access to: Azure AI Search (retrieval) Storage (documents, embeddings, outputs) Key Vault (secrets and connection strings) Inference workflows often span multiple services, each requiring: Correct DNS resolution Approved private endpoints Explicit outbound connectivity from the execution environment Under full isolation: Latency, reachability, and dependency access become tightly coupled to network configuration Identity and network failures often present as: “Model not reachable” “Timeout on retrieval step” “Search query fails intermittently” Misconfigured networking can break the application even when the model itself is healthy Result: At scale, network correctness becomes a prerequisite for application reliability, not just security control. Inference pipelines are no longer just model execution - they are distributed systems dependent on private connectivity between multiple services. Enterprise AI operations From an ops perspective: Networking defines the blast radius DNS and endpoint approval become operational prerequisites Portal and API behavior may differ under full isolation Supportability requires understanding control‑plane vs data‑plane separation Private networking hardens the platform - but also raises the bar for operational maturity. In practice, most of these operational considerations become visible first in inference workloads, where connectivity and dependency failures directly impact application behavior. Operational considerations in private Foundry deployments Across real enterprise deployments, common failure modes include: Private endpoint created but still pending approval DNS resolution still pointing to public endpoints instead of private IPs Managed outbound rules missing for: Storage Container Registry Model dependencies Compute can reach the workspace but not its data Certain portal or feature behaviors may differ under full isolation These are not isolated configuration issues – they are direct consequences of how network isolation makes dependencies explicit within the ML system. Troubleshooting: Experiencing these issues? The private endpoint diagnostic guide walks through resolution steps for each failure mode. Connectivity and access: separating security from reachability A key mental model: Private Endpoint is the security primitive. VPN, ExpressRoute, and peering are access patterns. Private Link secures the service endpoint itself. Enterprise connectivity determines who and what can reach it . In enterprise environments, inference traffic is rarely isolated to a single VNet. Instead, connectivity patterns such as ExpressRoute, VPN, and VNet peering determine how applications, users, and on-prem systems reliably reach private Foundry endpoints. Common patterns: Pattern Typical use case Private Endpoint + VNet Peering Azure app tier calling private RAG/inference services Private Endpoint + VPN Gateway Pilot or mid-scale customer environments needing hybrid access fast Private Endpoint + ExpressRoute Production enterprise AI platforms with strict connectivity and compliance needs Private Endpoint + ExpressRoute with VPN backup Mission-critical inference or internal copilots where private access must stay available This separation keeps security and connectivity concerns clean and auditable across distributed AI systems. Common enterprise patterns in private Foundry deployments: Private RAG systems: Application tiers operating within VNets, accessing Foundry, Search, Storage, and Key Vault through private connectivity Isolated evaluation pipelines: Evaluation workflows executing without public ingress or egress, ensuring controlled data movement and reproducible benchmarking Internal agents and copilots: Enterprise-facing inference systems with tightly governed access to internal data sources and dependent services Controlled training environments: Managed network boundaries with restricted outbound access to enforce compliance and dependency control These are not edge scenarios - they represent the emerging baseline for production-ready enterprise AI systems. Final takeaway: networking is part of the ML architecture In Microsoft Foundry, private networking decisions directly influence: Developer velocity and iteration cycles End-to-end inference reliability Evaluation consistency and reproducibility System observability and failure isolation Security and compliance posture Designing networking as a core part of the ML system enables reproducible, scalable, and compliant enterprise AI. Foundry provides the right primitives - but architectural clarity determines how those systems perform at scale. Next Steps Ready to implement private networking in your Foundry deployment? Here's where to go next: Get Started - Configure managed virtual networks in Microsoft Foundry - Step-by-step setup guide - Private endpoint configuration for Azure AI services - Detailed networking walkthrough Go Deeper - Microsoft Foundry networking architecture reference - Full architecture documentation - Troubleshooting private endpoint connectivity - Debug common failure modes covered in this post Connect with the Community - Share your private networking patterns in the comments below - Join the Microsoft Foundry Tech Community for ongoing discussions Have questions about a specific deployment pattern? Drop them in the comments - we're actively monitoring this thread.
headline_ja: Microsoft Foundryのprivate networking設計と推論基盤
overview: 本記事は、Microsoft Foundryにおけるprivate networkingを、単なるVNet設定ではなく、モデル開発・評価・提供・運用全体に影響する基盤設計として解説する。BYO VNetとManaged VNetの違い、Private Endpoint、Private DNS、outbound rules、Azure OpenAIやAzure AI Searchなど依存先との接続性を整理し、Preview/GA表記はなく、現行ドキュメントに基づく設計観点を示す。
whats_new: 新規機能の追加告知ではなく、Foundryのprivate networkingをどう設計すべきかを体系化した解説記事である。BYO VNetでは企業側がDNS、ルーティング、ファイアウォール、依存サービス到達性を細かく制御できる一方、Managed VNetではMicrosoft管理の境界内でPrivate EndpointとDNS解決を扱い、運用負荷を下げられる。どちらも private connectivity を提供するが、完全な顧客制御の有無が大きな違いとして明示されている。
key_takeaway: 金融・公共・製造のように「public exposureなし」が前提の組織では、Foundry導入可否はモデル性能よりもネットワーク到達性で決まる。本記事は、RAGやenterprise copilotで起きる失敗がDNS未設定やoutbound制限に起因し得ることを示し、BYO VNetかManaged VNetかをワークロード単位で選ぶべきだと示唆する。既存のネットワーク運用標準と整合させて設計することが重要。

title: Defense at AI speed: Microsoft’s new multi-model agentic security system tops leading industry benchmark
content: In this article AI-powered vulnerability discovery at hyper-scale Codename: MDASH—Microsoft Security’s new multi-model agentic scanning harness Using codename MDASH for security research The 5.12.2026 Patch Tuesday cohort Two deep dives CVE-2026-33827—Remote unauthenticated UAF in tcpip.sys via SSRR CVE-2026-33824: Unauthenticated IKEv2 SA_INIT + fragmentation → double-free → LocalSystem RCE How capable is codename MDASH? What this all means Conclusion Today Microsoft announced a major step forward in AI-powered cyber defense: our new agentic security system helped researchers find 16 new vulnerabilities across the Windows networking and authentication stack—including four Critical remote code execution flaws in components such as the Windows kernel TCP/IP stack and the IKEv2 service. They used the new Microsoft Security m ulti-mo d el a gentic s canning h arness (codename MDASH) which was built by Microsoft’s Autonomous Code Security team.
